evaluation_inputs.system_description: The Dynamic Map is a complex information system designed for urban traffic optimization. It consists of spatial databases storing static and dynamic data (road infrastructure, traffic conditions, events) and software modules for data collection from various sensors (video, acoustic, GPS), interpretation, and provision to clients (visualization, route planning, traffic control).

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Generic architectural views (ArchiMate) were available but lacked constructs to describe scenario influence on components or capture specific design decisions. Some specifications were missing or decisions had not yet been made.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: What is the platform for this component (e.g., PostgreSQL, Linux, IIS)?
Is the component logic fixed or customizable?
What is the query granularity (bulk, low, medium, high)?
What communication type is used (synchronous, asynchronous)?

hardware_view_description: Data originates from a network of fixed sensors (video detectors, acoustic sensors, inductive loops)
Data includes on-board GPS receivers installed in vehicles
End-user interaction via network or mobile interfaces
runtime_constraints: Real-time performance requirements for traffic analysis
High volume of data feeds from sensors
Interoperability with various sensor types and client modules

questions: How are transactions distributed across the distinct repositories (Static, Traffic, Event)?
How are ontology definitions synchronized with database dictionaries?
Is the query granularity optimized for bulk updates vs. individual reads?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: New sensor types and sensor processing software will be integrated in <6 days>
Dynamic Map will be capable of accepting feeds from up to 1000 sensors occurring every <60> sec
Dynamic Map will be capable of processing up to <200> read queries per second
New map features can be introduced and made discoverable with built-in functionality
Updating of data defining the road network can cause a loss of at most 10 values of a single monitoring parameter
Sensor failures will be detected in a configurable period ranging from 3 to 20 minutes
New event types and corresponding customizable detection routines can be introduced in 1 day
System will be able to accommodate and interpret location data from 5000 GPS tracker units updated every 5 sec
A Dynamic Map component can be ported to another platform within 20 days

business_goals: Optimize road traffic and provide tools for efficient traffic control
Enable fast response to road accidents or threats using real-time analysis
Provide efficient route planning and optimization based on dynamic traffic data
Integrate existing and new sensor infrastructure seamlessly
technical_constraints: Need for interoperability with diverse subsystems
architectural_drivers: Modifiability (Integration of new sensors and event types)

architectural_approaches.description: Central spatial databases (Static, Traffic, Event repositories) serve as the primary integration mechanism. All sensor data flows into these repositories, and client modules query them for information. Transaction distribution across distinct repositories allows parallel processing.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Centralized data access enables optimized query processing and bulk updates. Transaction distribution across distinct repositories (Static, Traffic, Event) allows parallel processing and reduces contention.
New sensor types can be integrated by adding data collection modules without affecting core repository structure. Ontology-based definitions enable extensibility for new event types and data formats.
Repository acts as a common integration point for heterogeneous sensor types (video, acoustic, GPS, inductive loops) and diverse client modules (visualization, route planning, traffic control).

architectural_approaches.description: System organized into distinct layers: data collection layer (sensors and acquisition modules), data storage layer (spatial repositories), data interpretation layer (processing and analysis modules), and presentation layer (client interfaces for visualization and control).

addressed_attributes.rationale: Layer separation allows changes in one layer (e.g., adding new sensor types or client modules) without affecting other layers. Clear interfaces between layers enable independent evolution.
Platform-specific concerns isolated to individual layers, enabling component porting across different platforms while maintaining system functionality.
Presentation layer can be modified independently to improve user interfaces without impacting underlying data processing or storage.

architectural_approaches.description: Sensors publish data updates at regular intervals (1000 fixed sensors at 60-second intervals, 5000 GPS trackers at 5-second intervals). Processing modules subscribe to relevant data streams for interpretation and event detection. Buffering mechanisms handle asynchronous communication.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Asynchronous communication and buffering mechanisms handle high-volume sensor feeds without blocking. Decoupled producers and consumers enable parallel processing of data streams.
Decoupled sensor feeds enable failure detection mechanisms (3-20 minute configurable detection window). System can continue operating even if individual sensors fail.
New subscribers can be added to process existing data streams without modifying sensor components. Event-driven architecture supports dynamic reconfiguration.

children.scenario_description: Dynamic Map will be capable of processing up to 200 read queries per second

children.scenario_description: New sensor types and sensor processing software will be integrated in <6 days

scenarios.scenario_text: Dynamic Map will be capable of accepting feeds from up to 1000 sensors occurring every 60 seconds during normal operations without data loss or processing delays
System will be able to accommodate and interpret location data from 5000 GPS tracker units updated every 5 seconds, maintaining real-time accuracy for traffic visualization and route planning

Dynamic Map will be capable of processing up to 200 read queries per second from client modules (visualization, route planning, traffic control) with response times suitable for real-time decision making
New sensor types and sensor processing software will be integrated into the system within 6 days, including data collection modules, repository schema updates, and client interface modifications
New event types and corresponding customizable detection routines can be introduced in 1 day using ontology-based definitions without requiring changes to core repository structure
New map features can be introduced and made discoverable with built-in functionality, allowing client modules to dynamically detect and utilize new capabilities without manual reconfiguration
Sensor failures will be detected in a configurable period ranging from 3 to 20 minutes through monitoring of expected periodic updates, enabling automated alerts and failover mechanisms

Updating of data defining the road network (static repository modifications) can cause a loss of at most 10 values of a single monitoring parameter during the transition period, ensuring data integrity
A Dynamic Map component can be ported to another platform within 20 days by isolating platform-specific dependencies to individual architectural layers

scenario_reference.text: Dynamic Map will be capable of accepting feeds from up to 1000 sensors occurring every 60 seconds during normal operations without data loss or processing delays

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with 1000 fixed sensors transmitting periodically
analysis_records.stimulus: 1000 sensor data feeds arriving every 60 seconds (approximately 16.7 sensor updates per second)
analysis_records.response_measure: No data loss or processing delays during continuous sensor ingestion
architectural_decisions: Data-Centric Repository with separate Traffic repository for sensor data
Publish-Subscribe pattern with buffering mechanisms for asynchronous sensor feeds
Transaction distribution across distinct repositories (Static, Traffic, Event) for parallel processing
analysis_records.reasoning: The relatively low update frequency (60-second intervals) and moderate sensor count (1000 units) results in approximately 16.7 updates per second. The Data-Centric Repository approach with buffering can handle this load through asynchronous processing. Transaction distribution across repositories reduces contention. However, without specific buffer sizes and database write throughput capacity, we cannot quantitatively verify that no data loss will occur during peak loads or bulk update scenarios.

non_risks.description: Low update frequency (60-second intervals) is well within typical database ingestion capabilities
non_risks.rationale: At 16.7 updates/second, even commodity database systems can handle this load. The publish-subscribe pattern with buffering provides additional headroom for temporary spikes.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Buffer size for sensor data ingestion queue
Database write transaction throughput capacity

scenario_reference.text: System will be able to accommodate and interpret location data from 5000 GPS tracker units updated every 5 seconds, maintaining real-time accuracy for traffic visualization and route planning

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with high-frequency GPS tracker updates
analysis_records.stimulus: 5000 GPS tracker units transmitting location data every 5 seconds (1000 updates per second)
analysis_records.response_measure: Real-time accuracy maintained for traffic visualization and route planning without processing backlog
architectural_decisions: Publish-Subscribe pattern with asynchronous buffering for GPS data streams
Separate Traffic repository to isolate high-volume GPS updates from other data
Data interpretation layer processes GPS streams for event detection and traffic analysis
analysis_records.reasoning: GPS trackers generate 1000 updates per second (5000 units / 5 seconds), which is significantly higher than fixed sensor load. The publish-subscribe pattern decouples data ingestion from processing, allowing buffering to smooth out bursts. However, sustained 1000 updates/second requires substantial database write capacity and network bandwidth. Without specific message sizes, network capacity, and database throughput metrics, this represents a significant performance risk.

risks.description: High-frequency GPS updates (1000/sec) may exceed database write capacity, causing processing backlog and delayed real-time accuracy
risks.rationale: Sustained 1000 writes per second requires high-performance database configuration. If write throughput is insufficient, buffers will fill and either data loss or increasing latency will occur. No quantitative verification of database capacity exists.

risks.description: Network bandwidth between GPS trackers and central repositories may become saturated
risks.rationale: Without knowing message sizes and available bandwidth, 1000 messages/second could exceed network capacity, especially if GPS data includes additional metadata beyond coordinates.

sensitivity_points.parameter: GPS message size (bytes per location update)
Network bandwidth capacity between GPS units and Traffic repository
Database write transaction rate (transactions per second)
Buffer capacity for GPS data ingestion queue

conflicting_attributes: Performance (real-time accuracy - positive with higher frequency)
Performance (system load and scalability - negative with higher frequency)

scenario_reference.text: Dynamic Map will be capable of processing up to 200 read queries per second from client modules (visualization, route planning, traffic control) with response times suitable for real-time decision making

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with concurrent client query load
analysis_records.stimulus: Up to 200 read queries per second from diverse client modules
analysis_records.response_measure: Response times suitable for real-time decision making (typically sub-second for interactive applications)
architectural_decisions: Data-Centric Repository with optimized query processing for spatial data
Transaction distribution across Static, Traffic, and Event repositories reduces query contention
Layered architecture separates presentation layer from data storage, enabling query optimization
analysis_records.reasoning: 200 read queries per second is a moderate load for well-designed database systems, especially with spatial indexing. The separation of repositories (Static, Traffic, Event) allows queries to be distributed, reducing contention. However, query complexity matters significantly - simple point queries are very different from complex spatial joins. Without specific query response time requirements or query complexity profiles, we cannot definitively assess whether 'suitable for real-time decision making' will be achieved.

risks.description: Complex spatial queries (e.g., route planning with real-time traffic) may exceed response time requirements under 200 queries/sec load
risks.rationale: Route planning queries likely involve complex spatial joins across Static (road network) and Traffic (current conditions) repositories. Without query optimization strategies (caching, materialized views, spatial indexing details), complex queries may take multiple seconds, unsuitable for real-time interaction.

non_risks.description: Simple read queries (e.g., current traffic at specific location) can be served efficiently at 200 queries/sec
non_risks.rationale: Point queries with spatial indexing typically execute in milliseconds. Repository separation reduces lock contention, allowing parallel query execution.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Query complexity distribution (simple vs. complex spatial queries)
Spatial indexing strategy and database configuration
Acceptable response time threshold for 'real-time decision making'

tradeoff_points.parameter: Repository normalization (separate Static, Traffic, Event repositories)
conflicting_attributes: Performance (reduced contention through transaction distribution - positive)
Performance (complex queries requiring joins across repositories - negative)

scenario_reference.text: New sensor types and sensor processing software will be integrated into the system within 6 days, including data collection modules, repository schema updates, and client interface modifications

analysis_records.environment: System evolution requiring integration of new sensor technology
analysis_records.stimulus: Request to add new sensor type with associated processing logic
analysis_records.response_measure: Complete integration (data collection, storage, processing, presentation) within 6 days
architectural_decisions: Layered architecture isolates sensor-specific concerns to data collection layer
Data-Centric Repository with extensible schema supports new data types
Ontology-based definitions enable semantic integration of new sensor data
Publish-Subscribe pattern allows new processing modules to subscribe to sensor feeds without modifying producers
analysis_records.reasoning: The layered architecture and publish-subscribe pattern provide strong modifiability support. New sensors can be added by creating data collection modules that publish to existing repositories, and new processing modules can subscribe to these feeds. However, 6 days is an aggressive timeline that requires well-defined interfaces, comprehensive documentation, and minimal coupling between components. Repository schema updates could require data migration and client interface modifications introduce cross-layer dependencies.

risks.description: Repository schema updates for new sensor data types may require data migration and extensive testing, potentially exceeding 6-day timeline
risks.rationale: Adding new columns or tables to spatial repositories with existing data requires careful migration planning. If schema changes affect existing queries or require index rebuilding, downtime and testing could extend beyond 6 days.

risks.description: Client interface modifications introduce cross-layer dependencies that may cascade changes beyond data collection layer
risks.rationale: If new sensor data requires presentation layer changes (new visualization types, user controls), the layered architecture's modifiability benefits are reduced. Changes propagate across multiple layers, increasing integration time.

non_risks.description: Adding new sensor data collection modules without schema changes can be accomplished quickly through publish-subscribe pattern
non_risks.rationale: If new sensor data fits existing repository schema (e.g., another GPS tracker type), only data collection module development is needed. Publish-subscribe decoupling allows independent deployment.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Degree of schema change required for new sensor type
Interface stability between architectural layers
Ontology extensibility for new sensor semantics

tradeoff_points.parameter: Repository schema flexibility (generic vs. sensor-specific schemas)
conflicting_attributes: Modifiability (adding new sensor types without schema changes - positive with generic schema)
Performance (query optimization for specific sensor types - negative with overly generic schema)

scenario_reference.text: Sensor failures will be detected in a configurable period ranging from 3 to 20 minutes through monitoring of expected periodic updates, enabling automated alerts and failover mechanisms

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with potential sensor hardware failures
analysis_records.stimulus: Sensor stops transmitting periodic updates due to hardware failure or network disconnection
analysis_records.response_measure: Failure detected within configurable 3-20 minute window, triggering automated alerts
architectural_decisions: Publish-Subscribe pattern with expected update intervals enables timeout-based failure detection
Decoupled sensor feeds allow system to continue operating despite individual sensor failures
Event repository can log failure events for monitoring and alerting
analysis_records.reasoning: The publish-subscribe architecture naturally supports failure detection through monitoring of expected periodic updates. Since sensors publish at known intervals (60 seconds for fixed sensors, 5 seconds for GPS), absence of updates within expected timeframes indicates failure. The 3-20 minute configurable window provides flexibility for different sensor types and network conditions. Decoupled architecture ensures individual sensor failures don't cascade to system-wide failures.

non_risks.description: Publish-subscribe pattern with known update intervals provides robust foundation for timeout-based failure detection
non_risks.rationale: Expected update intervals (60s for fixed sensors, 5s for GPS) are well-defined. Monitoring modules can easily detect missing updates within 3-20 minute window. Decoupled architecture prevents failure propagation.

non_risks.description: Configurable detection window (3-20 minutes) accommodates different sensor types and network reliability levels
non_risks.rationale: Flexibility in detection timing reduces false positives from temporary network issues while ensuring timely detection of genuine failures. Different sensor types can have different thresholds.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Failure detection window configuration (3-20 minute range)
sensitivity_points.correlated_attribute: Reliability (false positive rate vs. detection latency)

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (rapid failure detection - positive with shorter window)
Reliability (false positive reduction - positive with longer window)

scenario_reference.text: Updating of data defining the road network (static repository modifications) can cause a loss of at most 10 values of a single monitoring parameter during the transition period, ensuring data integrity

analysis_records.environment: Road network updates requiring static repository schema or data modifications
analysis_records.stimulus: Administrative update to road network definition (new roads, changed topology, updated attributes)
analysis_records.response_measure: Maximum 10 values of a single monitoring parameter lost during update transition
architectural_decisions: Separation of Static repository (road network) from Traffic repository (dynamic sensor data)
Transaction isolation mechanisms to manage concurrent reads during schema updates
Buffering of sensor data during repository updates to minimize data loss
analysis_records.reasoning: The separation of Static and Traffic repositories allows road network updates to occur with limited impact on ongoing sensor data collection. However, if road network topology changes (e.g., road segments renumbered), sensor data referencing old topology may become orphaned. The 10-value loss limit suggests a brief transition period where data mapping is ambiguous. Without specific transaction management and data migration strategies, this requirement is at risk.

risks.description: Road network topology changes may orphan more than 10 sensor values if referential integrity mechanisms are insufficient
risks.rationale: If road segments are renumbered or removed, sensor data referencing those segments may be lost or misattributed. Without documented data migration procedures or referential integrity constraints, the 10-value limit may be exceeded during complex topology changes.

risks.description: Concurrent sensor updates during Static repository modifications may cause data inconsistency beyond 10-value threshold
risks.rationale: If 1000 sensors update every 60 seconds and 5000 GPS units update every 5 seconds, during a multi-second repository update window, hundreds of sensor values could reference inconsistent road network state. Transaction isolation strategy is critical but not specified.

non_risks.description: Repository separation limits scope of data loss to parameters directly dependent on modified road network elements
non_risks.rationale: Traffic and Event repositories remain operational during Static repository updates. Only sensor data explicitly referencing modified road segments is at risk, limiting potential data loss.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Transaction isolation level during Static repository updates
Duration of road network update transaction window
sensitivity_points.correlated_attribute: Reliability (number of sensor values at risk)

sensitivity_points.parameter: Referential integrity enforcement between Static and Traffic repositories

tradeoff_points.parameter: Repository normalization (separate Static and Traffic repositories)
conflicting_attributes: Reliability (referential integrity during concurrent updates - negative)

scenario_reference.text: New event types and corresponding customizable detection routines can be introduced in 1 day using ontology-based definitions without requiring changes to core repository structure

analysis_records.environment: System evolution requiring new event detection capabilities
analysis_records.stimulus: Request to add new event type (e.g., new traffic incident category) with custom detection logic
analysis_records.response_measure: Complete integration of new event type and detection routine within 1 day
architectural_decisions: Ontology-based event definitions enable semantic extensibility without schema changes
Event repository designed with flexible schema to accommodate new event types
Data interpretation layer supports pluggable detection routines that subscribe to sensor data streams
Publish-subscribe pattern allows new detection modules to be added without modifying data sources
analysis_records.reasoning: The 1-day timeline is achievable if the ontology-based approach is well-implemented and detection routines can be added as independent modules. The publish-subscribe pattern supports this by allowing new subscribers (detection routines) to consume existing sensor data streams. However, this assumes detection logic can be implemented, tested, and deployed rapidly, and that ontology extensions don't require complex semantic reasoning or validation.

non_risks.description: Ontology-based event definitions with flexible Event repository schema enable rapid addition of new event types
non_risks.rationale: If Event repository uses extensible schema (e.g., key-value attributes, JSON documents), new event types can be added without schema migration. Ontology provides semantic definitions without code changes.

non_risks.description: Publish-subscribe pattern allows new detection routines to be deployed as independent subscribers without modifying sensor data sources
non_risks.rationale: Detection modules can subscribe to existing Traffic and Event data streams, process data according to new logic, and publish detected events. Decoupled architecture enables rapid deployment.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Ontology extension complexity (simple attribute addition vs. complex semantic relationships)
Detection routine complexity (simple threshold-based vs. complex pattern recognition)

scenario_reference.text: New map features can be introduced and made discoverable with built-in functionality, allowing client modules to dynamically detect and utilize new capabilities without manual reconfiguration

analysis_records.environment: System evolution with new visualization or analysis features
analysis_records.stimulus: Introduction of new map feature (e.g., new data layer, visualization mode, or analysis capability)
analysis_records.response_measure: Client modules automatically discover and utilize new features without manual reconfiguration
architectural_decisions: Layered architecture with well-defined interfaces between presentation and data layers
Service discovery or capability advertisement mechanism for dynamic feature detection
Ontology-based feature definitions enable semantic discovery of new capabilities
analysis_records.reasoning: Dynamic feature discovery requires a metadata or service registry mechanism where new features can advertise their capabilities and client modules can query available features. This is architecturally feasible but requires additional infrastructure beyond the basic Data-Centric Repository and Layered patterns. Without explicit service discovery mechanisms documented in the architectural approaches, this scenario represents a potential gap.

risks.description: No explicit service discovery or capability advertisement mechanism documented in architectural approaches
risks.rationale: While ontology-based definitions support semantic extensibility, dynamic feature discovery requires runtime metadata exchange between presentation layer and data/interpretation layers. Without documented service registry or capability query interfaces, client modules cannot automatically discover new features.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Service discovery mechanism implementation (registry-based, query-based, or advertisement-based)
Feature metadata richness (simple availability flags vs. detailed capability descriptions)
sensitivity_points.correlated_attribute: Usability (client ability to utilize discovered features effectively)

conflicting_attributes: Modifiability (automatic client adaptation to new features - positive)
Performance (runtime overhead of capability queries and metadata exchange - negative)

scenario_reference.text: A Dynamic Map component can be ported to another platform within 20 days by isolating platform-specific dependencies to individual architectural layers

analysis_records.environment: System deployment on new hardware or software platform
analysis_records.stimulus: Request to port a system component (e.g., data collection module, repository, or client interface) to different platform
analysis_records.response_measure: Complete porting including adaptation, testing, and deployment within 20 days
architectural_decisions: Layered architecture isolates platform-specific concerns to individual layers
Well-defined interfaces between layers enable independent platform adaptation
Data-Centric Repository abstracts storage mechanisms from application logic
analysis_records.reasoning: The layered architecture provides good portability support by isolating platform dependencies. For example, porting a client interface to a new GUI framework affects only the presentation layer, while porting a repository to a new database platform affects only the data storage layer. The 20-day timeline is reasonable for single-component porting with well-defined interfaces. However, portability depends heavily on interface stability and the degree of platform-specific code in each layer.

risks.description: Cross-layer dependencies or platform-specific optimizations may require changes beyond the target component layer
risks.rationale: If components use platform-specific features for performance optimization (e.g., database-specific spatial queries, OS-specific threading), porting may require changes across multiple layers. Without documented interface contracts and platform abstraction layers, 20-day timeline is at risk.

non_risks.description: Layered architecture with clear separation of concerns supports component-level portability
non_risks.rationale: If platform-specific code is truly isolated to individual layers and interfaces are platform-agnostic, porting a single component requires only adaptation of that layer. Well-defined interfaces enable independent testing and deployment.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Degree of platform-specific optimization in component implementation
Interface abstraction level between layers (platform-agnostic vs. platform-exposed)

conflicting_attributes: Performance (leveraging platform-specific features for optimization - positive)
Portability (ease of porting to different platforms - negative)
